project.name: Cedar Storefront
project.description: A deliberately small API used to demonstrate proof-carrying release checks.

claims.title: Order creation requires an authenticated customer
claims.whyItMatters: An unauthenticated order endpoint enables fraud, inventory abuse, and privacy failures.

evidence.description: The request boundary rejects callers before order data is created.
evidence.detail: Found the expected implementation pattern in fixtures/storefront/api.mjs.

evidence.description: Executable tests exercise the anonymous, malformed, and authenticated paths.
evidence.output:
✔ rejects anonymous order creation (1.425917ms)
✔ returns a safe error for malformed orders (0.908625ms)
✔ accepts an authenticated, valid order (0.153ms)
ℹ tests 3
ℹ suites 0
ℹ pass 3
ℹ fail 0
ℹ cancelled 0
ℹ skipped 0
ℹ todo 0
ℹ duration_ms 193.645041

claims.title: Public error responses never serialize server secrets
claims.whyItMatters: Agent-written error handling often leaks configuration or implementation details.

evidence.description: The public API uses a stable error code rather than returning thrown objects.
evidence.detail: Found the expected implementation pattern in fixtures/storefront/api.mjs.

evidence.description: A narrow static guard catches a high-impact secret-exposure pattern.
evidence.detail: Did not find the forbidden implementation pattern in fixtures/storefront/api.mjs.

claims.title: Every release claim is connected to reproducible evidence
claims.whyItMatters: A green test suite alone does not tell a reviewer which user promise the tests establish.

evidence.description: The configuration requires each claim to explain the user or business risk it protects.
evidence.detail: Found the expected implementation pattern in verity.config.json.

evidence.description: A reviewer must confirm that the evidence still covers the intended release scope.
evidence.detail: Human confirmation required. Verity records this explicitly instead of pretending an automated check proves it.
